{{Short description| part_of of Kofun-period Kofuns in Ōita, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | native_name =川部・高森古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = 赤塚古墳01.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Akazuka Kofun within the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|33|32|33|N|131|21|57|E|region:JP-44|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Usa, Ōita]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun custer | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.3rd to 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|32|33|N|131|21|57|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster'''|川部・高森古墳群|}} is a group [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Kyozuka, Takamori neighborhood of the city of [[Usa, Ōita]], on the island of [[Kyushu]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/2dd590|title=川部・高森古墳群群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kawabe Takamori Kofun cluster is located on a plateau on the right bank of the Yakan River, which flows from south to north in Usa City, and consists of six keyhole-shaped {{nihongo|''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above., as well as around 120 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style round tombs and surrounding ditch tombs. It is the largest tumulus group in Oita Prefecture, and the second largest in Kyushu after the [[Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], which consists of approximately 320 tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
There is no regularity in the arrangement of the six keyhole-shaped tombs, and their axial directions vary. Many of the surrounding Kofuns and ditch tombs cannot be immediately identified as Kofuns because their mounds have disappeared. It is speculated that the people buried in these keyhole-shaped tumuli are thought to be chiefs who ruled the Usa region during the Kofun period, perhaps members of the Usa clan, and the surrounding tumuli and surrounding ditch graves are the graves of those family members and their vassals. The fact that Kofuns were continuously constructed in the same area between the 3rd and 6th centuries suggests that stable rule was in place for a long period of time.
The area is now preserved as the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park, which is approximately 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[Usa Station]]. Some of the artifacts excavated from the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster are on display at the branch of the [[:en:Ōita Prefectural Museum of History|Ōita Prefectural Museum of History]] located within the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These were collectively designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/000dd649|title=大分県免ヶ平古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
===Akatsuka Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Akatsuka Kofun'''|赤塚古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 57.5 meters, a posterior diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.8 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 2.5 meters. It is said to be the oldest keyhole-shaped tumulus in Kyushu, built at the end of the 3rd century. It has an dry moat around it with a width of 8.5 meters to dd meters. The stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a box-type sarcophagus. During [[archaeological excavation]]s in 1921, [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as four triangular-rimmed divine beast [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, one triangular-rimmed dragon and tiger bronze mirror, a jasper ball, an iron sword piece, and an iron ax were unearthed. The bronze mirrors are said to be the same as those excavated from [[Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Ishizukayama Kofun]] (Fukuoka Prefecture), and [[Haraguchi Kofun]] (same), and were given to the chiefs of various regions by the early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato monarchy]].
===Mengadaira Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Mengadaira Kofun'''|免ヶ平古墳|}} is currently in the shape of a round tomb with a diameter of 30.5 meters and a height of 4 meters, but it is estimated that it was originally a keyhole-shaped ''[[zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of about 50 meters. An dry moat has been dug around the mound. A split bamboo-shaped wooden coffin was placed in a pit-style stone burial chamber made of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]] in the center of the posterior circle, and grave goods included a diagonal-rimmed bronze mirror with two gods and two beasts, a triangular-rimmed bronze mirror with three gods and three beasts, a ''[[magatama]]'' made of jadeite, and an iron sword, iron spears, axes, and daggers have been unearthed. In addition, the bones of a young woman, a diagonal-edge bronze mirror of two gods and two beasts, a ''magatama'' made of jadeite, a jasper bead made of jasper, a stone chime, and a sword were unearthed from a box-shaped sarcophagus housed in a secondary burial the southern part of the posterior circular portion. It is estimated that the tumulus was built in the latter half of the 4th century.
===Fukushoji Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Fukushoji Kofun'''|福勝寺古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 78 meters, a posterior diameter of 54 meters and height of 7.5 meters, an anterior width of 22 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is the largest keyhole-shaped tumulus in this group of ''kofun'' and the fourth largest in Oita Prefecture. It is estimated to have been constructed in the first half of the 5th century. It is also called the {{nihongo|Kasugayama Kofun|春日山古墳||}}. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kurumazaka Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Kurumazaka Kofun'''|車坂古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 58 meters, a posterial diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.5 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun. It has a dry moat ranging from dd meters to 22 meters wide from the east to the south. Next to the tumulus is a small round tumulus called the Jizōdō Kofun, which is possibly a ''baizuka'' ancillary tomb. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kakubō Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Kakubō Kofun'''|角房古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 46 meters, a posterior diameter of 30 meters and height of 4 meters, and an anterior width of 18 meters. It is located in a place facing the Yakan River. Part of the front part has disappeared. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun and Kurumazaka Kofun. It dry an empty moat around it with a width of 7 to 12 meters. The interior has not been investigated.
===Tsurumi Kofun===
The {{nihongo|'''Tsurumi Kofun'''|鶴見古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 31 meters, a posterior diameter of 21 meters and height of 3 meters, an anterior width of 17 meters and a height of 2 meters. It has a short and wide anterior portion, which is characteristic of late keyhole-shaped tumuli, and is estimated to be the last keyhole-shaped tumulus built in the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster, built in the mid-6th century. The interior is a one-sleeve, horizontal cave-style stone burial chamber with a depth of 2.8 meters, a width of 2 meters, and a ceiling height of 2.5 meters, and can be viewed. Among the grave goods excavated were small glass beads, copper chimes, earrings, iron arrowheads, swords, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], and [[Sue pottery]] pottery.
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]

During [[archaeological excavation]]s in 1921, [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as four triangular-rimmed divine beast [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, one triangular-rimmed dragon and tiger bronze mirror, a jasper ball, an iron sword piece, and an iron ax were unearthed. The bronze mirrors are said to be the same as those excavated from [[Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Ishizukayama Kofun]] (Fukuoka Prefecture), and [[Haraguchi Kofun]] (same), and were given to the chiefs of various regions by the early [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato monarchy]].
===Mengadaira Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Mengadaira Kofun'''|免ヶ平古墳|}} is currently in the shape of a round tomb with a diameter of 30.5 meters and a height of 4 meters, but it is estimated that it was originally a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of about 50 meters. An dry moat has been dug around the mound. A split bamboo-shaped wooden coffin was placed in a pit-style stone burial chamber made of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]] in the center of the posterior circle, and grave goods included a diagonal-rimmed bronze mirror with two gods and two beasts, a triangular-rimmed bronze mirror with three gods and three beasts, a ''[[magatama]]'' made of jadeite, and an iron sword, iron spears, axes, and daggers have been unearthed. In addition, the bones of a young woman, a diagonal-edge bronze mirror of two gods and two beasts, a ''magatama'' made of jadeite, a jasper bead made of jasper, a stone chime, and a sword were unearthed from a box-shaped sarcophagus housed in a secondary burial the southern part of the posterior circular portion. It is estimated that the tumulus was built in the latter half of the 4th century.
===Fukushoji Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Fukushoji Kofun'''|福勝寺古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 78 meters, a posterior diameter of 54 meters and height of 7.5 meters, an anterior width of 22 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is the largest keyhole-shaped tumulus in this group of ''kofun'' and the fourth largest in Oita Prefecture. It is estimated to have been constructed in the first half of the 5th century. It is also called the {{nihongo|Kasugayama Kofun|春日山古墳||}}. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kurumazaka Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Kurumazaka Kofun'''|車坂古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 58 meters, a posterial diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.5 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun. It has a dry moat ranging from dd meters to 22 meters wide from the east to the south. Next to the tumulus is a small round tumulus called the Jizōdō Kofun, which is possibly a ''baizuka'' ancillary tomb. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kakubō Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Kakubō Kofun'''|角房古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 46 meters, a posterior diameter of 30 meters and height of 4 meters, and an anterior width of 18 meters. It is located in a place facing the Yakan River. Part of the front part has disappeared. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun and Kurumazaka Kofun. It dry an empty moat around it with a width of 7 to 12 meters. The interior has not been investigated.
===Tsurumi Kofun===
The {{nihongo|'''Tsurumi Kofun'''|鶴見古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 31 meters, a posterior diameter of 21 meters and height of 3 meters, an anterior width of 17 meters and a height of 2 meters. It has a short and wide anterior portion, which is characteristic of late keyhole-shaped tumuli, and is estimated to be the last keyhole-shaped tumulus built in the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster, built in the mid-6th century. The interior is a one-sleeve, horizontal cave-style stone burial chamber with a depth of 2.8 meters, a width of 2 meters, and a ceiling height of 2.5 meters, and can be viewed. Among the grave goods excavated were small glass beads, copper chimes, earrings, iron arrowheads, swords, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], and [[Sue pottery]] pottery.

{{Short description| part_of of Kofun-period Kofuns in Ōita, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | native_name =川部・高森古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = 赤塚古墳01.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Akazuka Kofun within the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|33|32|33|N|131|21|57|E|region:JP-44|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Usa, Ōita]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun custer | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.3rd to 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|32|33|N|131|21|57|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster'''|川部・高森古墳群|}} is a group [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Kyozuka, Takamori neighborhood of the city of [[Usa, Ōita]], on the island of [[Kyushu]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/2dd590|title=川部・高森古墳群群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kawabe Takamori Kofun cluster is located on a plateau on the right bank of the Yakan River, which flows from south to north in Usa City, and consists of six keyhole-shaped {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above., as well as around 120 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style round tombs and surrounding ditch tombs. It is the largest tumulus group in Oita Prefecture, and the second largest in Kyushu after the [[Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], which consists of approximately 320 tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
There is no regularity in the arrangement of the six keyhole-shaped tombs, and their axial directions vary. Many of the surrounding Kofuns and ditch tombs cannot be immediately identified as Kofuns because their mounds have disappeared. It is speculated that the people buried in these keyhole-shaped tumuli are thought to be chiefs who ruled the Usa region during the Kofun period, perhaps members of the Usa clan, and the surrounding tumuli and surrounding ditch graves are the graves of those family members and their vassals. The fact that Kofuns were continuously constructed in the same area between the 3rd and 6th centuries suggests that stable rule was in place for a long period of time.
The area is now preserved as the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park, which is approximately 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[Usa Station]]. Some of the artifacts excavated from the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster are on display at the branch of the [[:en:Ōita Prefectural Museum of History|Ōita Prefectural Museum of History]] located within the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These were collectively designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/000dd649|title=大分県免ヶ平古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
===Akatsuka Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Akatsuka Kofun'''|赤塚古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 57.5 meters, a posterior diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.8 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 2.5 meters. It is said to be the oldest keyhole-shaped tumulus in Kyushu, built at the end of the 3rd century. It has an dry moat around it with a width of 8.5 meters to dd meters. The stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a box-type sarcophagus. During [[archaeological excavation]]s in 1921, [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as four triangular-rimmed divine beast [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, one triangular-rimmed dragon and tiger bronze mirror, a jasper ball, an iron sword piece, and an iron ax were unearthed. The bronze mirrors are said to be the same as those excavated from [[Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Ishizukayama Kofun]] (Fukuoka Prefecture), and [[Haraguchi Kofun]] (same), and were given to the chiefs of various regions by the early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato monarchy]].
===Mengadaira Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Mengadaira Kofun'''|免ヶ平古墳|}} is currently in the shape of a round tomb with a diameter of 30.5 meters and a height of 4 meters, but it is estimated that it was originally a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of about 50 meters. An dry moat has been dug around the mound. A split bamboo-shaped wooden coffin was placed in a pit-style stone burial chamber made of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]] in the center of the posterior circle, and grave goods included a diagonal-rimmed bronze mirror with two gods and two beasts, a triangular-rimmed bronze mirror with three gods and three beasts, a ''[[magatama]]'' made of jadeite, and an iron sword, iron spears, axes, and daggers have been unearthed. In addition, the bones of a young woman, a diagonal-edge bronze mirror of two gods and two beasts, a ''magatama'' made of jadeite, a jasper bead made of jasper, a stone chime, and a sword were unearthed from a box-shaped sarcophagus housed in a secondary burial the southern part of the posterior circular portion. It is estimated that the tumulus was built in the latter half of the 4th century.
===Fukushoji Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Fukushoji Kofun'''|福勝寺古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 78 meters, a posterior diameter of 54 meters and height of 7.5 meters, an anterior width of 22 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is the largest keyhole-shaped tumulus in this group of ''kofun'' and the fourth largest in Oita Prefecture. It is estimated to have been constructed in the first half of the 5th century. It is also called the {{nihongo|Kasugayama Kofun|春日山古墳||}}. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kurumazaka Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Kurumazaka Kofun'''|車坂古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 58 meters, a posterial diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.5 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun. It has a dry moat ranging from dd meters to 22 meters wide from the east to the south. Next to the tumulus is a small round tumulus called the Jizōdō Kofun, which is possibly a ''baizuka'' ancillary tomb. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kakubō Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Kakubō Kofun'''|角房古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 46 meters, a posterior diameter of 30 meters and height of 4 meters, and an anterior width of 18 meters. It is located in a place facing the Yakan River. Part of the front part has disappeared. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun and Kurumazaka Kofun. It dry an empty moat around it with a width of 7 to 12 meters. The interior has not been investigated.
===Tsurumi Kofun===
The {{nihongo|'''Tsurumi Kofun'''|鶴見古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 31 meters, a posterior diameter of 21 meters and height of 3 meters, an anterior width of 17 meters and a height of 2 meters. Among the grave goods excavated were small glass beads, copper chimes, earrings, iron arrowheads, swords, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], and [[Sue pottery]] pottery.

revision.text:
{{Short description| part_of of Kofun-period Kofuns in Ōita, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | native_name =川部・高森古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = 赤塚古墳01.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Akazuka Kofun within the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|33|32|33|N|131|21|57|E|region:JP-44|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Usa, Ōita|Usa, Ōita]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun custer | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.3rd to 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|32|33|N|131|21|57|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster'''|川部・高森古墳群|}} is a group [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Kyozuka, Takamori neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Usa, Ōita|Usa, Ōita]], on the island of [[Kyushu]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/2dd590|title=川部・高森古墳群群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kawabe Takamori Kofun cluster is located on a plateau on the right bank of the Yakan River, which flows from south to north in Usa City, and consists of six keyhole-shaped {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above., as well as around 120 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style round tombs and surrounding ditch tombs. It is the largest tumulus group in Oita Prefecture, and the second largest in Kyushu after the [[Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], which consists of approximately 320 tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
There is no regularity in the arrangement of the six keyhole-shaped tombs, and their axial directions vary. Many of the surrounding Kofuns and ditch tombs cannot be immediately identified as Kofuns because their mounds have disappeared. It is speculated that the people buried in these keyhole-shaped tumuli are thought to be chiefs who ruled the Usa region during the Kofun period, perhaps members of the Usa clan, and the surrounding tumuli and surrounding ditch graves are the graves of those family members and their vassals. The fact that Kofuns were continuously constructed in the same area between the 3rd and 6th centuries suggests that stable rule was in place for a long period of time.
The area is now preserved as the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park, which is approximately 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Usa Station|Usa Station]]. Some of the artifacts excavated from the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster are on display at the branch of the [[:en:Ōita Prefectural Museum of History|Ōita Prefectural Museum of History]] located within the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These were collectively designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/000dd649|title=大分県免ヶ平古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
===Akatsuka Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Akatsuka Kofun'''|赤塚古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 57.5 meters, a posterior diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.8 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 2.5 meters. It is said to be the oldest keyhole-shaped tumulus in Kyushu, built at the end of the 3rd century. It has an dry moat around it with a width of 8.5 meters to dd meters. The stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a box-type sarcophagus. During [[archaeological excavation]]s in 1921, [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as four triangular-rimmed divine beast [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, one triangular-rimmed dragon and tiger bronze mirror, a jasper ball, an iron sword piece, and an iron ax were unearthed. The bronze mirrors are said to be the same as those excavated from [[Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Ishizukayama Kofun]] (Fukuoka Prefecture), and [[Haraguchi Kofun]] (same), and were given to the chiefs of various regions by the early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato monarchy]].
===Mengadaira Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Mengadaira Kofun'''|免ヶ平古墳|}} is currently in the shape of a round tomb with a diameter of 30.5 meters and a height of 4 meters, but it is estimated that it was originally a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of about 50 meters. An dry moat has been dug around the mound. A split bamboo-shaped wooden coffin was placed in a pit-style stone burial chamber made of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]] in the center of the posterior circle, and grave goods included a diagonal-rimmed bronze mirror with two gods and two beasts, a triangular-rimmed bronze mirror with three gods and three beasts, a ''[[magatama]]'' made of jadeite, and an iron sword, iron spears, axes, and daggers have been unearthed. In addition, the bones of a young woman, a diagonal-edge bronze mirror of two gods and two beasts, a ''magatama'' made of jadeite, a jasper bead made of jasper, a stone chime, and a sword were unearthed from a box-shaped sarcophagus housed in a secondary burial the southern part of the posterior circular portion. It is estimated that the tumulus was built in the latter half of the 4th century.
===Fukushoji Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Fukushoji Kofun'''|福勝寺古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 78 meters, a posterior diameter of 54 meters and height of 7.5 meters, an anterior width of 22 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is the largest keyhole-shaped tumulus in this group of ''kofun'' and the fourth largest in Oita Prefecture. It is estimated to have been constructed in the first half of the 5th century. It is also called the {{nihongo|Kasugayama Kofun|春日山古墳||}}. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kurumazaka Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Kurumazaka Kofun'''|車坂古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 58 meters, a posterial diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.5 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun. It has a dry moat ranging from dd meters to 22 meters wide from the east to the south. Next to the tumulus is a small round tumulus called the Jizōdō Kofun, which is possibly a ''baizuka'' ancillary tomb. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kakubō Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Kakubō Kofun'''|角房古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 46 meters, a posterior diameter of 30 meters and height of 4 meters, and an anterior width of 18 meters. It is located in a place facing the Yakan River. Part of the front part has disappeared. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun and Kurumazaka Kofun. It dry an empty moat around it with a width of 7 to 12 meters. The interior has not been investigated.
===Tsurumi Kofun===
The {{nihongo|'''Tsurumi Kofun'''|鶴見古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 31 meters, a posterior diameter of 21 meters and height of 3 meters, an anterior width of 17 meters and a height of 2 meters. It has a short and wide anterior portion, which is characteristic of late keyhole-shaped tumuli, and is estimated to be the last keyhole-shaped tumulus built in the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster, built in the mid-6th century. The interior is a one-sleeve, horizontal cave-style stone burial chamber with a depth of 2.8 meters, a width of 2 meters, and a ceiling height of 2.5 meters, and can be viewed. Among the grave goods excavated were small glass beads, copper chimes, earrings, iron arrowheads, swords, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], and [[Sue pottery]] pottery.
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]

The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/2dd590|title=川部・高森古墳群群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kawabe Takamori Kofun cluster is located on a plateau on the right bank of the Yakan River, which flows from south to north in Usa City, and consists of six keyhole-shaped {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above., as well as around 120 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style round tombs and surrounding ditch tombs. It is the largest tumulus group in Oita Prefecture, and the second largest in Kyushu after the [[Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], which consists of approximately 320 tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
There is no regularity in the arrangement of the six keyhole-shaped tombs, and their axial directions vary. Many of the surrounding Kofuns and ditch tombs cannot be immediately identified as Kofuns because their mounds have disappeared. It is speculated that the people buried in these keyhole-shaped tumuli are thought to be chiefs who ruled the Usa region during the Kofun period, perhaps members of the Usa clan, and the surrounding tumuli and surrounding ditch graves are the graves of those family members and their vassals. The fact that Kofuns were continuously constructed in the same area between the 3rd and 6th centuries suggests that stable rule was in place for a long period of time.
The area is now preserved as the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park, which is approximately 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Usa Station|Usa Station]]. Some of the artifacts excavated from the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster are on display at the branch of the [[:en:Ōita Prefectural Museum of History|Ōita Prefectural Museum of History]] located within the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These were collectively designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/000dd649|title=大分県免ヶ平古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
===Akatsuka Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Akatsuka Kofun'''|赤塚古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 57.5 meters, a posterior diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.8 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 2.5 meters. It is said to be the oldest keyhole-shaped tumulus in Kyushu, built at the end of the 3rd century. It has an dry moat around it with a width of 8.5 meters to dd meters. The stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a box-type sarcophagus. During [[archaeological excavation]]s in 1921, [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as four triangular-rimmed divine beast [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, one triangular-rimmed dragon and tiger bronze mirror, a jasper ball, an iron sword piece, and an iron ax were unearthed. The bronze mirrors are said to be the same as those excavated from [[Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Ishizukayama Kofun]] (Fukuoka Prefecture), and [[Haraguchi Kofun]] (same), and were given to the chiefs of various regions by the early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato monarchy]].
===Mengadaira Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Mengadaira Kofun'''|免ヶ平古墳|}} is currently in the shape of a round tomb with a diameter of 30.5 meters and a height of 4 meters, but it is estimated that it was originally a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of about 50 meters. An dry moat has been dug around the mound. A split bamboo-shaped wooden coffin was placed in a pit-style stone burial chamber made of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]] in the center of the posterior circle, and grave goods included a diagonal-rimmed bronze mirror with two gods and two beasts, a triangular-rimmed bronze mirror with three gods and three beasts, a ''[[magatama]]'' made of jadeite, and an iron sword, iron spears, axes, and daggers have been unearthed. In addition, the bones of a young woman, a diagonal-edge bronze mirror of two gods and two beasts, a ''magatama'' made of jadeite, a jasper bead made of jasper, a stone chime, and a sword were unearthed from a box-shaped sarcophagus housed in a secondary burial the southern part of the posterior circular portion. It is estimated that the tumulus was built in the latter half of the 4th century.
===Fukushoji Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Fukushoji Kofun'''|福勝寺古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 78 meters, a posterior diameter of 54 meters and height of 7.5 meters, an anterior width of 22 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is the largest keyhole-shaped tumulus in this group of ''kofun'' and the fourth largest in Oita Prefecture. It is estimated to have been constructed in the first half of the 5th century. It is also called the {{nihongo|Kasugayama Kofun|春日山古墳||}}. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kurumazaka Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Kurumazaka Kofun'''|車坂古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 58 meters, a posterial diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.5 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun. It has a dry moat ranging from dd meters to 22 meters wide from the east to the south. Next to the tumulus is a small round tumulus called the Jizōdō Kofun, which is possibly a ''baizuka'' ancillary tomb. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kakubō Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Kakubō Kofun'''|角房古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 46 meters, a posterior diameter of 30 meters and height of 4 meters, and an anterior width of 18 meters. It is located in a place facing the Yakan River. Part of the front part has disappeared. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun and Kurumazaka Kofun. It dry an empty moat around it with a width of 7 to 12 meters. The interior has not been investigated.
===Tsurumi Kofun===
The {{nihongo|'''Tsurumi Kofun'''|鶴見古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 31 meters, a posterior diameter of 21 meters and height of 3 meters, an anterior width of 17 meters and a height of 2 meters. It has a short and wide anterior portion, which is characteristic of late keyhole-shaped tumuli, and is estimated to be the last keyhole-shaped tumulus built in the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster, built in the mid-6th century. The interior is a one-sleeve, horizontal cave-style stone burial chamber with a depth of 2.8 meters, a width of 2 meters, and a ceiling height of 2.5 meters, and can be viewed. Among the grave goods excavated were small glass beads, copper chimes, earrings, iron arrowheads, swords, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery.
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]

The stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a box-type sarcophagus. During [[archaeological excavation]]s in 1921, [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as four triangular-rimmed divine beast [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, one triangular-rimmed dragon and tiger bronze mirror, a jasper ball, an iron sword piece, and an iron ax were unearthed. The bronze mirrors are said to be the same as those excavated from [[Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Ishizukayama Kofun]] (Fukuoka Prefecture), and [[Haraguchi Kofun]] (same), and were given to the chiefs of various regions by the early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato monarchy]].
===Mengadaira Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Mengadaira Kofun'''|免ヶ平古墳|}} is currently in the shape of a round tomb with a diameter of 30.5 meters and a height of 4 meters, but it is estimated that it was originally a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of about 50 meters. An dry moat has been dug around the mound. A split bamboo-shaped wooden coffin was placed in a pit-style stone burial chamber made of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]] in the center of the posterior circle, and grave goods included a diagonal-rimmed bronze mirror with two gods and two beasts, a triangular-rimmed bronze mirror with three gods and three beasts, a ''[[magatama]]'' made of jadeite, and an iron sword, iron spears, axes, and daggers have been unearthed. In addition, the bones of a young woman, a diagonal-edge bronze mirror of two gods and two beasts, a ''magatama'' made of jadeite, a jasper bead made of jasper, a stone chime, and a sword were unearthed from a box-shaped sarcophagus housed in a secondary burial the southern part of the posterior circular portion. It is estimated that the tumulus was built in the latter half of the 4th century.
===Fukushoji Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Fukushoji Kofun'''|福勝寺古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 78 meters, a posterior diameter of 54 meters and height of 7.5 meters, an anterior width of 22 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is the largest keyhole-shaped tumulus in this group of ''kofun'' and the fourth largest in Oita Prefecture. It is estimated to have been constructed in the first half of the 5th century. It is also called the {{nihongo|Kasugayama Kofun|春日山古墳||}}. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kurumazaka Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Kurumazaka Kofun'''|車坂古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 58 meters, a posterial diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.5 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun. It has a dry moat ranging from dd meters to 22 meters wide from the east to the south. Next to the tumulus is a small round tumulus called the Jizōdō Kofun, which is possibly a ''baizuka'' ancillary tomb. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kakubō Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Kakubō Kofun'''|角房古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 46 meters, a posterior diameter of 30 meters and height of 4 meters, and an anterior width of 18 meters. It is located in a place facing the Yakan River. Part of the front part has disappeared. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun and Kurumazaka Kofun. It dry an empty moat around it with a width of 7 to 12 meters. The interior has not been investigated.
===Tsurumi Kofun===
The {{nihongo|'''Tsurumi Kofun'''|鶴見古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 31 meters, a posterior diameter of 21 meters and height of 3 meters, an anterior width of 17 meters and a height of 2 meters. It has a short and wide anterior portion, which is characteristic of late keyhole-shaped tumuli, and is estimated to be the last keyhole-shaped tumulus built in the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster, built in the mid-6th century. The interior is a one-sleeve, horizontal cave-style stone burial chamber with a depth of 2.8 meters, a width of 2 meters, and a ceiling height of 2.5 meters, and can be viewed. Among the grave goods excavated were small glass beads, copper chimes, earrings, iron arrowheads, swords, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11479328]] Wikidata name: Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11479328]] Wikidata name: Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description| part_of of Kofun-period Kofuns in Ōita, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | native_name =川部・高森古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = 赤塚古墳01.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Akazuka Kofun within the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{coord|33|32|33|N|131|21|57|E|region:JP-44|display=inline, title}}; {{Coord|33.54186388888889|N|131.36090277777777|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Usa, Ōita|Usa, Ōita]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun custer | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.3rd to 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|qid=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|32|33|N|131|21|57|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster'''|川部・高森古墳群|}} is a group [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Kyozuka, Takamori neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Usa, Ōita|Usa, Ōita]], on the island of [[Kyushu]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/2dd590|title=川部・高森古墳群群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kawabe Takamori Kofun cluster is located on a plateau on the right bank of the Yakan River, which flows from south to north in Usa City, and consists of six keyhole-shaped {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above., as well as around 120 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style round tombs and surrounding ditch tombs. It is the largest tumulus group in Oita Prefecture, and the second largest in Kyushu after the [[Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], which consists of approximately 320 tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
There is no regularity in the arrangement of the six keyhole-shaped tombs, and their axial directions vary. Many of the surrounding Kofuns and ditch tombs cannot be immediately identified as Kofuns because their mounds have disappeared. It is speculated that the people buried in these keyhole-shaped tumuli are thought to be chiefs who ruled the Usa region during the Kofun period, perhaps members of the Usa clan, and the surrounding tumuli and surrounding ditch graves are the graves of those family members and their vassals. The fact that Kofuns were continuously constructed in the same area between the 3rd and 6th centuries suggests that stable rule was in place for a long period of time.
The area is now preserved as the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park, which is approximately 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Usa Station|Usa Station]]. Some of the artifacts excavated from the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster are on display at the branch of the [[:en:Ōita Prefectural Museum of History|Ōita Prefectural Museum of History]] located within the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These were collectively designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/000dd649|title=大分県免ヶ平古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>

It is said to be the oldest keyhole-shaped tumulus in Kyushu, built at the end of the 3rd century. It has an dry moat around it with a width of 8.5 meters to dd meters. The stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a box-type sarcophagus. During [[archaeological excavation]]s in 1921, [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as four triangular-rimmed divine beast [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, one triangular-rimmed dragon and tiger bronze mirror, a jasper ball, an iron sword piece, and an iron ax were unearthed. The bronze mirrors are said to be the same as those excavated from [[Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Ishizukayama Kofun]] (Fukuoka Prefecture), and [[Haraguchi Kofun]] (same), and were given to the chiefs of various regions by the early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato monarchy]].

An dry moat has been dug around the mound. A split bamboo-shaped wooden coffin was placed in a pit-style stone burial chamber made of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]] in the center of the posterior circle, and grave goods included a diagonal-rimmed bronze mirror with two gods and two beasts, a triangular-rimmed bronze mirror with three gods and three beasts, a ''[[magatama]]'' made of jadeite, and an iron sword, iron spears, axes, and daggers have been unearthed. In addition, the bones of a young woman, a diagonal-edge bronze mirror of two gods and two beasts, a ''magatama'' made of jadeite, a jasper bead made of jasper, a stone chime, and a sword were unearthed from a box-shaped sarcophagus housed in a secondary burial the southern part of the posterior circular portion. It is estimated that the tumulus was built in the latter half of the 4th century.
===Fukushoji Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Fukushoji Kofun'''|福勝寺古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 78 meters, a posterior diameter of 54 meters and height of 7.5 meters, an anterior width of 22 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is the largest keyhole-shaped tumulus in this group of ''kofun'' and the fourth largest in Oita Prefecture. It is estimated to have been constructed in the first half of the 5th century. It is also called the {{nihongo|Kasugayama Kofun|春日山古墳||}}. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kurumazaka Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Kurumazaka Kofun'''|車坂古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 58 meters, a posterial diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.5 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun. It has a dry moat ranging from dd meters to 22 meters wide from the east to the south. Next to the tumulus is a small round tumulus called the Jizōdō Kofun, which is possibly a ''baizuka'' ancillary tomb. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kakubō Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Kakubō Kofun'''|角房古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 46 meters, a posterior diameter of 30 meters and height of 4 meters, and an anterior width of 18 meters. It is located in a place facing the Yakan River. Part of the front part has disappeared. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun and Kurumazaka Kofun. It dry an empty moat around it with a width of 7 to 12 meters. The interior has not been investigated.
===Tsurumi Kofun===
The {{nihongo|'''Tsurumi Kofun'''|鶴見古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 31 meters, a posterior diameter of 21 meters and height of 3 meters, an anterior width of 17 meters and a height of 2 meters. The interior is a one-sleeve, horizontal cave-style stone burial chamber with a depth of 2.8 meters, a width of 2 meters, and a ceiling height of 2.5 meters, and can be viewed. Among the grave goods excavated were small glass beads, copper chimes, earrings, iron arrowheads, swords, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery.

It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun. It has a dry moat ranging from dd meters to 22 meters wide from the east to the south. Next to the tumulus is a small round tumulus called the Jizōdō Kofun, which is possibly a ''baizuka'' ancillary tomb. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kakubō Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Kakubō Kofun'''|角房古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 46 meters, a posterior diameter of 30 meters and height of 4 meters, and an anterior width of 18 meters. It is located in a place facing the Yakan River. Part of the front part has disappeared. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun and Kurumazaka Kofun. It dry an empty moat around it with a width of 7 to 12 meters. The interior has not been investigated.
===Tsurumi Kofun===
The {{nihongo|'''Tsurumi Kofun'''|鶴見古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 31 meters, a posterior diameter of 21 meters and height of 3 meters, an anterior width of 17 meters and a height of 2 meters. It has a short and wide anterior portion, which is characteristic of late keyhole-shaped tumuli, and is estimated to be the last keyhole-shaped tumulus built in the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster, built in the mid-6th century. The interior is a one-sleeve, horizontal cave-style stone burial chamber with a depth of 2.8 meters, a width of 2 meters, and a ceiling height of 2.5 meters, and can be viewed. Among the grave goods excavated were small glass beads, copper chimes, earrings, iron arrowheads, swords, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster]][[en:Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster]][[ja:川部・高森古墳群]][[zh:川部和高森古墳群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11479328]] Wikidata name: Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11479328]] Wikidata name: Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description| part_of of Kofun-period Kofuns in Ōita, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | native_name =川部・高森古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = 赤塚古墳01.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Akazuka Kofun within the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{coord|33|32|33|N|131|21|57|E|region:JP-44|display=inline, title}}; {{Coord|33.54186388888889|N|131.36090277777777|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Usa, Ōita|Usa, Ōita]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun custer | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.3rd to 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|qid=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|32|33|N|131|21|57|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster'''|川部・高森古墳群|}} is a group [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Kyozuka, Takamori neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Usa, Ōita|Usa, Ōita]], on the island of [[Kyushu]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/2dd590|title=川部・高森古墳群群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kawabe Takamori Kofun cluster is located on a plateau on the right bank of the Yakan River, which flows from south to north in Usa City, and consists of six keyhole-shaped {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above., as well as around 120 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style round tombs and surrounding ditch tombs. It is the largest tumulus group in Oita Prefecture, and the second largest in Kyushu after the [[Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], which consists of approximately 320 tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
There is no regularity in the arrangement of the six keyhole-shaped tombs, and their axial directions vary. Many of the surrounding Kofuns and ditch tombs cannot be immediately identified as Kofuns because their mounds have disappeared. It is speculated that the people buried in these keyhole-shaped tumuli are thought to be chiefs who ruled the Usa region during the Kofun period, perhaps members of the Usa clan, and the surrounding tumuli and surrounding ditch graves are the graves of those family members and their vassals. The fact that Kofuns were continuously constructed in the same area between the 3rd and 6th centuries suggests that stable rule was in place for a long period of time.
The area is now preserved as the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park, which is approximately 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Usa Station|Usa Station]]. Some of the artifacts excavated from the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster are on display at the branch of the [[:en:Ōita Prefectural Museum of History|Ōita Prefectural Museum of History]] located within the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These were collectively designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/000dd649|title=大分県免ヶ平古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
===Akatsuka Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Akatsuka Kofun'''|赤塚古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 57.5 meters, a posterior diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.8 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 2.5 meters. It is said to be the oldest keyhole-shaped tumulus in Kyushu, built at the end of the 3rd century. It has an dry moat around it with a width of 8.5 meters to dd meters. The stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a box-type sarcophagus. During [[archaeological excavation]]s in 1921, [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as four triangular-rimmed divine beast [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, one triangular-rimmed dragon and tiger bronze mirror, a jasper ball, an iron sword piece, and an iron ax were unearthed. The bronze mirrors are said to be the same as those excavated from [[Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Ishizukayama Kofun]] (Fukuoka Prefecture), and [[Haraguchi Kofun]] (same), and were given to the chiefs of various regions by the early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato monarchy]].
===Mengadaira Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Mengadaira Kofun'''|免ヶ平古墳|}} is currently in the shape of a round tomb with a diameter of 30.5 meters and a height of 4 meters, but it is estimated that it was originally a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of about 50 meters. An dry moat has been dug around the mound. A split bamboo-shaped wooden coffin was placed in a pit-style stone burial chamber made of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]] in the center of the posterior circle, and grave goods included a diagonal-rimmed bronze mirror with two gods and two beasts, a triangular-rimmed bronze mirror with three gods and three beasts, a ''[[magatama]]'' made of jadeite, and an iron sword, iron spears, axes, and daggers have been unearthed. In addition, the bones of a young woman, a diagonal-edge bronze mirror of two gods and two beasts, a ''magatama'' made of jadeite, a jasper bead made of jasper, a stone chime, and a sword were unearthed from a box-shaped sarcophagus housed in a secondary burial the southern part of the posterior circular portion. It is estimated that the tumulus was built in the latter half of the 4th century.
===Fukushoji Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Fukushoji Kofun'''|福勝寺古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 78 meters, a posterior diameter of 54 meters and height of 7.5 meters, an anterior width of 22 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is the largest keyhole-shaped tumulus in this group of ''kofun'' and the fourth largest in Oita Prefecture. It is estimated to have been constructed in the first half of the 5th century. It is also called the {{nihongo|Kasugayama Kofun|春日山古墳||}}. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kurumazaka Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Kurumazaka Kofun'''|車坂古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 58 meters, a posterial diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.5 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun. It has a dry moat ranging from dd meters to 22 meters wide from the east to the south. Next to the tumulus is a small round tumulus called the Jizōdō Kofun, which is possibly a ''baizuka'' ancillary tomb. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kakubō Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Kakubō Kofun'''|角房古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 46 meters, a posterior diameter of 30 meters and height of 4 meters, and an anterior width of 18 meters. It is located in a place facing the Yakan River. Part of the front part has disappeared. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun and Kurumazaka Kofun. It dry an empty moat around it with a width of 7 to 12 meters. The interior has not been investigated.
===Tsurumi Kofun===
The {{nihongo|'''Tsurumi Kofun'''|鶴見古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 31 meters, a posterior diameter of 21 meters and height of 3 meters, an anterior width of 17 meters and a height of 2 meters. It has a short and wide anterior portion, which is characteristic of late keyhole-shaped tumuli, and is estimated to be the last keyhole-shaped tumulus built in the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster, built in the mid-6th century. The interior is a one-sleeve, horizontal cave-style stone burial chamber with a depth of 2.8 meters, a width of 2 meters, and a ceiling height of 2.5 meters, and can be viewed. Among the grave goods excavated were small glass beads, copper chimes, earrings, iron arrowheads, swords, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery.

{{Coord|33.54186388888889|N|131.36090277777777|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Usa, Ōita|Usa, Ōita]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun custer | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.3rd to 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|32|33|N|131|21|57|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster'''|川部・高森古墳群|}} is a group [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Kyozuka, Takamori neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Usa, Ōita|Usa, Ōita]], on the island of [[Kyushu]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/2dd590|title=川部・高森古墳群群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kawabe Takamori Kofun cluster is located on a plateau on the right bank of the Yakan River, which flows from south to north in Usa City, and consists of six keyhole-shaped {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above., as well as around 120 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style round tombs and surrounding ditch tombs. It is the largest tumulus group in Oita Prefecture, and the second largest in Kyushu after the [[Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], which consists of approximately 320 tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
There is no regularity in the arrangement of the six keyhole-shaped tombs, and their axial directions vary. Many of the surrounding Kofuns and ditch tombs cannot be immediately identified as Kofuns because their mounds have disappeared. It is speculated that the people buried in these keyhole-shaped tumuli are thought to be chiefs who ruled the Usa region during the Kofun period, perhaps members of the Usa clan, and the surrounding tumuli and surrounding ditch graves are the graves of those family members and their vassals. The fact that Kofuns were continuously constructed in the same area between the 3rd and 6th centuries suggests that stable rule was in place for a long period of time.
The area is now preserved as the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park, which is approximately 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Usa Station|Usa Station]]. Some of the artifacts excavated from the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster are on display at the branch of the [[:en:Ōita Prefectural Museum of History|Ōita Prefectural Museum of History]] located within the Usa Fudoki no Oka Park.<ref name="Shiseki"/> These were collectively designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1980.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/000dd649|title=大分県免ヶ平古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
===Akatsuka Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Akatsuka Kofun'''|赤塚古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 57.5 meters, a posterior diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.8 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 2.5 meters. It is said to be the oldest keyhole-shaped tumulus in Kyushu, built at the end of the 3rd century. It has an dry moat around it with a width of 8.5 meters to dd meters. The stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a box-type sarcophagus. During [[archaeological excavation]]s in 1921, [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as four triangular-rimmed divine beast [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, one triangular-rimmed dragon and tiger bronze mirror, a jasper ball, an iron sword piece, and an iron ax were unearthed. The bronze mirrors are said to be the same as those excavated from [[Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Ishizukayama Kofun]] (Fukuoka Prefecture), and [[Haraguchi Kofun]] (same), and were given to the chiefs of various regions by the early [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato monarchy]].
===Mengadaira Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Mengadaira Kofun'''|免ヶ平古墳|}} is currently in the shape of a round tomb with a diameter of 30.5 meters and a height of 4 meters, but it is estimated that it was originally a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of about 50 meters. An dry moat has been dug around the mound. A split bamboo-shaped wooden coffin was placed in a pit-style stone burial chamber made of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]] in the center of the posterior circle, and grave goods included a diagonal-rimmed bronze mirror with two gods and two beasts, a triangular-rimmed bronze mirror with three gods and three beasts, a ''[[magatama]]'' made of jadeite, and an iron sword, iron spears, axes, and daggers have been unearthed. In addition, the bones of a young woman, a diagonal-edge bronze mirror of two gods and two beasts, a ''magatama'' made of jadeite, a jasper bead made of jasper, a stone chime, and a sword were unearthed from a box-shaped sarcophagus housed in a secondary burial the southern part of the posterior circular portion. It is estimated that the tumulus was built in the latter half of the 4th century.
===Fukushoji Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Fukushoji Kofun'''|福勝寺古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 78 meters, a posterior diameter of 54 meters and height of 7.5 meters, an anterior width of 22 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is the largest keyhole-shaped tumulus in this group of ''kofun'' and the fourth largest in Oita Prefecture. It is estimated to have been constructed in the first half of the 5th century. It is also called the {{nihongo|Kasugayama Kofun|春日山古墳||}}. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kurumazaka Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Kurumazaka Kofun'''|車坂古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 58 meters, a posterial diameter of 36 meters and height of 4.5 meters, an anterior width of 21 meters and height of 3.5 meters. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun. It has a dry moat ranging from dd meters to 22 meters wide from the east to the south. Next to the tumulus is a small round tumulus called the Jizōdō Kofun, which is possibly a ''baizuka'' ancillary tomb. The interior has not been investigated.
===Kakubō Kofun === The {{nihongo|'''Kakubō Kofun'''|角房古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 46 meters, a posterior diameter of 30 meters and height of 4 meters, and an anterior width of 18 meters. It is located in a place facing the Yakan River. Part of the front part has disappeared. It is estimated that it was built in the 5th century, following Fukushoji Kofun and Kurumazaka Kofun. It dry an empty moat around it with a width of 7 to 12 meters. The interior has not been investigated.
===Tsurumi Kofun===
The {{nihongo|'''Tsurumi Kofun'''|鶴見古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]'' with a total length of 31 meters, a posterior diameter of 21 meters and height of 3 meters, an anterior width of 17 meters and a height of 2 meters. It has a short and wide anterior portion, which is characteristic of late keyhole-shaped tumuli, and is estimated to be the last keyhole-shaped tumulus built in the Kawabe-Takamori Kofun cluster, built in the mid-6th century. The interior is a one-sleeve, horizontal cave-style stone burial chamber with a depth of 2.8 meters, a width of 2 meters, and a ceiling height of 2.5 meters, and can be viewed. Among the grave goods excavated were small glass beads, copper chimes, earrings, iron arrowheads, swords, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery.
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]
